meta.title: ref vs reactive for Vue state shape
meta.summary: A Vue component manages several related form and filter fields. Would you keep each value in its own ref or group them in a reactive object?

scenario: You are building a Vue 3 search panel for an admin page. The panel has a query string, selected status, selected assignee, sort order, and an `onlyEscalated` toggle. The component resets these fields together, sends them together in one search request, and restores them from a saved draft when the user returns to the page. One teammate wants to keep every field in its own `ref` because that feels explicit and easy to read. Another teammate wants to group the fields in one `reactive` object because the values move together as one piece of state. In the interview, the panel asks which direction you would defend.
prompt: Which direction would you defend for this prompt, and how would you explain when the other option is still the better answer?

options.label: Use separate refs
options.summary: Fits best when values are independent, small in number, or easier to reason about as separate pieces of state.
whenItWins: The values are mostly independent and do not naturally move as one grouped object.
The component only has a few fields, and separate refs keep the state shape very direct.
You want to pass around or expose individual values from a composable without wrapping everything in one object.
watchOutFor: As related fields grow and start resetting, restoring, and submitting together, many separate refs can make grouped operations noisier than they need to be.
A weak answer picks refs only because they feel simpler, without asking whether the state already behaves like one coherent object.

options.label: Use a reactive object
options.summary: Fits best when several related fields behave like one draft, filter object, or form-shaped state boundary.
whenItWins: The fields are conceptually one unit, such as a search filter object, form draft, or settings payload.
The component resets, restores, patches, or submits the values together rather than treating them as unrelated values.
You want the code to reflect that these fields belong to one coherent state shape.
watchOutFor: A reactive object can be weaker if the state is tiny or if the values are actually independent and easier to work with individually.
A weak answer says reactive is always better for objects without checking whether the grouped shape really improves clarity.

decisionMatrix.title: Small set of independent values
decisionMatrix.prompt: A few values are updated separately and do not really form one meaningful object boundary.

cells.note: This is easier to defend because separate refs keep independent values simple and direct.
Still possible, but now you should explain why grouping the values improves clarity instead of just changing syntax.

decisionMatrix.title: The exact prompt in this battle
decisionMatrix.prompt: Several filter fields reset, restore, and submit together as one search state object.

cells.note: Possible, but harder to defend because grouped operations start spreading across several separate refs.
This is the cleanest answer because the state already behaves like one coherent filter object.

decisionMatrix.title: Composable exposing independent pieces of state
decisionMatrix.prompt: A composable needs to expose several values that consumers often read and update separately.

cells.note: This becomes easier to defend when the values are consumed as separate signals rather than one form-shaped object.
It can still work, but you should explain why consumers benefit from the grouped object instead of individual refs.

evaluationDimensions.title: Do these values behave like one object or several separate signals?
evaluationDimensions.description: This is the first question. The decision should come from the shape of the state, not from which API looks cleaner at first glance.

evaluationDimensions.title: Are the fields reset, restored, or submitted together?
evaluationDimensions.description: Grouped operations are a strong signal that the values may want to live as one reactive object.

evaluationDimensions.title: Which choice keeps the component easiest to read?
evaluationDimensions.description: A strong answer should care about whether the code communicates one draft object or a set of unrelated values.

evaluationDimensions.title: How will other code consume this state?
evaluationDimensions.description: Sometimes individual refs are cleaner because other parts of the code really do want separate values rather than one grouped structure.

evaluationDimensions.title: Are you choosing based on boundary or on habit?
evaluationDimensions.description: Many answers become weak when they treat ref or reactive as a universal default instead of matching the API to the actual state shape.

strongAnswer.title: A strong answer here would lean reactive because the prompt describes one coherent filter state object
strongAnswer.summary: This search panel is not just holding a few random values. The fields reset together, restore together, and get sent together as one payload. That makes a reactive object the easier answer to defend for the exact prompt.
reasoning: The key clue is state shape. These values are not behaving like unrelated signals. They are acting like one filter draft.
That makes `reactive` a natural fit because the code can express one grouped filter object instead of scattering grouped operations across several refs.
I would still use separate refs when the values are truly independent, very small in number, or exposed separately from a composable where individual ownership is clearer.
strongAnswer.recommendation: For this exact prompt, I would keep the search filters in one reactive object and use separate refs only for values that are genuinely independent rather than part of the grouped filter state.

interviewerPushback.question: Isn't ref the simpler default in Vue 3?
interviewerPushback.answer: It can be for independent values, and I use it often. But this prompt is about a grouped filter draft that resets and submits as one unit, so the state shape itself points more clearly toward a reactive object.

interviewerPushback.question: When would separate refs clearly be the better answer?
interviewerPushback.answer: When the values are mostly independent, there are only a few of them, or consumers really want separate pieces of state rather than one grouped object.

interviewerPushback.question: What would change your answer?
interviewerPushback.answer: If the filter values stopped behaving like one draft object and instead became a small set of independent values used separately across the component or composable, I would move back toward individual refs.

answerExamples.title: Weak answer
answerExamples.answer: I would use reactive because it is better for objects.
answerExamples.whyItWorks: It does not. This sounds generic and teaches no boundary for when separate refs are still the clearer tool.

answerExamples.title: Decent answer
answerExamples.answer: I would probably use reactive because the filters belong together and get submitted together.
answerExamples.whyItWorks: This is directionally fine, but it still needs a sharper explanation of state shape and when refs remain the better choice.

answerExamples.title: Strong answer
answerExamples.answer: For this prompt I would use one reactive object because the query, filters, sort, and toggle behave like one search draft. The component resets them together, restores them together, and sends them together in one request, so I want the state shape to reflect that. I would still use separate refs for a small number of independent values or when a composable needs to expose separate pieces of state more directly.
answerExamples.whyItWorks: This sounds strong because it starts from how the state behaves, not from API preference, and it still gives a clear boundary for when refs are the better fit.

answerFramework: Start by asking whether the values behave like one grouped state object or several separate pieces of state.
Check whether the code resets, restores, or submits those values together.
Choose the API that matches that state shape.
Explain the main cost of the other option, either scattered grouped operations or unnecessary grouping.
End with a simple rule of thumb, for example: refs for separate signals, reactive for one coherent object-shaped state boundary.
antiPatterns: Treating ref as the default answer for every piece of state.
Treating reactive as automatically better whenever an object exists.
Ignoring whether the fields actually behave as one grouped draft.
Talking only about syntax preference instead of state shape and operations.
